primary_diagnosis.name: Thyrotoxic crisis precipitated by intercurrent infection and abrupt anti-thyroid medication cessation, with new-onset atrial fibrillation

pathophysiology_summary: In a patient with underlying Graves' disease, abrupt cessation of thionamide therapy removes the brake on thyroid hormone synthesis while an intercurrent infection acts as an acute stressor, tipping compensated thyrotoxicosis into thyroid storm — a life-threatening, decompensated hypermetabolic state. Surging free T3/T4 amplify catecholamine sensitivity, producing hyperpyrexia, extreme tachyarrhythmia (here new atrial fibrillation) out of proportion to the fever, CNS dysfunction (agitation, delirium, hallucinations), and gastrointestinal hyperactivity (nausea, diarrhoea) with volume loss. The uncontrolled hypermetabolism and adrenergic overdrive drive the heart toward high-output cardiac failure; untreated, thyroid storm carries a high mortality. Definitive therapy is a beta-blocker for adrenergic control, a thionamide to block new hormone synthesis, an iodine preparation (after the thionamide) to block hormone release, corticosteroid to reduce peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion, active cooling and treatment of the precipitant — all requiring emergency in-hospital care.

differential.evidence_basis: Hyperpyrexia, extreme tachycardia, agitation/delirium and profuse sweating in a patient with a known overactive thyroid who stopped her tablets.
High fever after a recent chest infection — must be considered and treated; also a recognised precipitant of thyroid storm, so not mutually exclusive.
Hyperthermia, tachycardia and agitation can mimic a stimulant toxidrome; weighed down by the known thyroid history and absence of any substance-use history.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Early on, thyroid storm should be at or near the top given the thyroid history; sepsis must be co-considered because of the preceding infection. Anchoring solely on infection is the trap.

differential.evidence_basis: Tachycardia out of proportion to fever, hand tremor, GI hyperactivity, plus abrupt anti-thyroid drug cessation with an infective precipitant — the Burch-Wartofsky constellation.
Remains an active concurrent diagnosis and precipitant; empiric infection work-up and treatment proceed in parallel.
Hyperthermia with agitation and autonomic instability — excluded by the absence of a culprit serotonergic/antipsychotic drug and by the thyroid history.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The out-of-proportion tachycardia plus the drug-cessation history are the pivot points that move thyroid storm to a confident lead while keeping sepsis co-managed.

differential.diagnosis: Thyroid storm precipitated by infection and thionamide cessation, with new atrial fibrillation
differential.evidence_basis: Diagnostic tetrad — hyperpyrexia, disproportionate tachyarrhythmia, CNS dysfunction and GI dysfunction — in decompensated Graves' disease; ED confirms with an undetectable TSH and markedly raised free T3/T4.
Treated as the trigger of the storm, not an alternative to it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The final formulation is thyroid storm WITH a precipitating infection — not one or the other. Automatic fail if the emergency is not recognised and escalated.

red_flags.clinical_significance: A cardinal feature of thyroid storm; the degree of pyrexia is a major weighted component of the Burch-Wartofsky score and drives hypermetabolic collapse.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to recognise a hypermetabolic emergency; delayed cooling and definitive therapy.

red_flags.description: Severe tachycardia (~155 bpm) out of proportion to fever, with an irregularly irregular pulse suggesting new atrial fibrillation
red_flags.clinical_significance: Adrenergic overdrive with tachyarrhythmia is pushing toward high-output cardiac failure — the immediate cardiovascular threat to life.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised impending high-output failure and haemodynamic collapse.

red_flags.description: Acute CNS dysfunction — severe agitation, delirium and hallucinations
red_flags.clinical_significance: CNS involvement marks severe (Burch-Wartofsky high) thyroid storm and carries a self-harm and injury risk in the acute setting.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-triage of a severe-storm marker; safety risk to the agitated patient.

red_flags.description: Abrupt cessation of anti-thyroid (carbimazole) and beta-blocker (propranolol) therapy about a month ago
red_flags.clinical_significance: Removes the brake on hormone synthesis and adrenergic control — the key precipitant that, with infection, tipped Graves' disease into storm.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing the precipitant obscures the diagnosis and the prevention message.

red_flags.description: Thyrotoxic examination signs — palpable goitre with audible bruit, fine resting tremor and brisk deep tendon reflexes
red_flags.clinical_significance: Confirm an intrinsically thyroid aetiology at the bedside; unavailable via telehealth and therefore sealed, but decisive on in-person or ED assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: None at the telehealth stage — these are clinician-elicited and inform the sealed answer key only.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the whole picture to the preceding chest infection and stopping at 'febrile, tachycardic, recent infection = sepsis', missing that the tachycardia is out of proportion to the fever and that thyroid storm is the decompensated primary event (with the infection as its trigger).
Reading the agitation, hallucinations and screaming as a primary psychiatric or panic presentation, rather than as delirium from a hypermetabolic endocrine crisis.
Reaching for aspirin to bring the fever down — salicylates displace thyroid hormone from binding proteins, raise free hormone and worsen the storm. Cooling plus paracetamol is correct.
Attempting to manage a decompensated endocrine emergency remotely instead of recognising it and escalating to an emergency ambulance for beta-blockade, thionamide, steroid and cooling.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Highly suggestive of thyroid storm (score well above 45: hyperpyrexia + severe CNS dysfunction + tachyarrhythmia/AF + GI dysfunction + a precipitant)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: A score of 45 or more is highly suggestive of thyroid storm and mandates immediate emergency treatment.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Exact blood pressure, heart rate and respiratory rate on a validated monitor
Confirmation of an irregularly irregular rhythm / atrial fibrillation (requires ECG)
Palpation of the goitre and auscultation of a thyroid bruit
Deep tendon reflexes and formal neurological examination
Thyroid function tests (undetectable TSH, markedly elevated free T3 and free T4) and a septic screen
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 Priority 1 ambulance for continuous cardiac monitoring and emergency transport; coach the carer through external cooling, a quiet low-stimulation environment and safe (non-forcible) supervision while awaiting paramedics. Definitive beta-blockade, thionamide, iodine, corticosteroid and cooling occur in hospital.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The real risk is UNDER-action or mis-anchoring: managing at home, labelling it a panic attack, or treating only for sepsis and missing the thyroid storm. The AI Doctor cannot confirm the diagnosis remotely but must treat this as a time-critical emergency and escalate.

guidelines_referenced.reference: American Thyroid Association Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis (2016)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Thyroid storm is a clinical diagnosis requiring immediate treatment; management combines a beta-adrenergic blocker, a thionamide, an inorganic iodide (given after the thionamide), corticosteroids, active cooling and treatment of the precipitating illness in a critical-care setting.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Burch HB, Wartofsky L. Life-threatening thyrotoxicosis: thyroid storm (1993)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: A point scale (temperature, CNS effects, GI-hepatic dysfunction, tachycardia/atrial fibrillation, congestive heart failure and a precipitant) stratifies the likelihood of thyroid storm; a score of 45 or more is highly suggestive.
